en.extract:
The Tucumán amazon (Amazona tucumana), also known as the Tucumán parrot, alder amazon, or alder parrot, is a vulnerable species of bird in subfamily Arinae of the family Psittacidae, the African and New World parrots. It is found in Argentina and Bolivia.


Taxonomy and systematics
The Tucumán amazon and the red-spectacled amazon (A. pretrei) have been considered conspecific but are now treated as sister species. The Tucuman amazon is monotypic.


Description
The Tucumán amazon is about 31 cm (12 in) long and weighs 250 to 280 g (8.8 to 9.9 oz). It is mostly green, with black edges on the body feathers that give a scaly appearance. Its forehead and sometimes its lores are red. Bare white skin surrounds its eye. Its lower thighs are orange-yellow, its primary coverts red, and its undertail coverts yellow-green. Its primaries have blue tips and its tail feathers yellowish tips. Immature birds have entirely green thighs.


Distribution and habitat
The Tucumán amazon is found from Santa Cruz and Chuquisaca departments in Bolivia south into Argentina as far as Catamarca Province. It inhabits the Yungas bioregion in montane woodland characterized by pure stands of alder (Alnus acuminata) or Podocarpus parlatorei. In elevation it mostly ranges between 1,600 and 2,600 m (5,200 and 8,500 ft) but occurs as low as 500 m (1,600 ft).


Behavior


Movement
The Tucumán amazon moves seasonally between higher elevations and, in August to October, as low as 500 m (1,600 ft) at the Chaco/Yungas ecotone.


Feeding
A study in Argentina found that almost a quarter of the Tucumán amazon's diet was the fruits and seeds of Podocarpus parlatorei; most of the rest was taken from six other plants. Winter food was dominated by Acacia seeds. Flowers and fruits of both native and introduced plants were eaten.


Breeding
The Tucumán amazon nests between November and January. It uses cavities in large trees, often those excavated by woodpeckers. In the Argentina study, the average clutch was 3.6 eggs and ranged from one to five. The incubation period was 26 to 30 days and fledging occurred 50 to 58 days after hatch. In another study, nest success was related to the availability of Podocarpus parlatorei fruits.


Vocalization
The Tucumán amazon makes a "variety of screeches and calls, including shrill shrieks and lower-pitched barks." Its flight call is "a repeated, somewhat yelping, shrill "quiowk"."


Status
The IUCN originally assessed the Tucumán amazon in 2004 as being of Least Concern, then in 2005 as Near Threatened, and since 2011 as Vulnerable. It has a somewhat limited range and its estimated population of fewer than 15,000 mature individuals is believed to be decreasing. "The species is susceptible to trapping for trade, which is considered the main driver of rapid population declines". "Habitat in Argentina is highly degraded and consists of small, isolated fragments." It is considered locally common but generally uncommon. It occurs in several protected areas, but less than 25% of its preferred habitat is protected.


References


Further reading
Forshaw, Joseph M. (2006). Parrots of the World; an Identification Guide. Illustrated by Frank Knight. Princeton University Press. ISBN 0-691-09251-6.

The '''Tucumán amazon''' ('''''Amazona tucumana'''''), also known as the '''Tucumán parrot''', '''alder amazon''', or '''alder parrot''', is a [[vulnerable species]] of bird in subfamily [[Arinae]] of the family [[Psittacidae]], the African and New World parrots. It is found in Argentina and Bolivia.<ref name=IOC13.1>{{cite web |url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/new/bow/parrots/ |title=Parrots, cockatoos |website=IOC World Bird List |version =v 13.1 |editor-last1=Gill |editor-first1= F. |editor-last2=Donsker|editor-first2=D.|editor-last3=Rasmussen |editor-first3=P. |date=January 2023 |access-date=18 February 2023 }}</ref><ref name=IUCN/>

==Taxonomy and systematics==

The Tucumán amazon and the [[red-spectacled amazon]] (''A. pretrei'') have been considered [[conspecific]] but are now treated as [[sister species]].<ref name=SACClist>Remsen, J. V., Jr., J. I. Areta, E. Bonaccorso, S. Claramunt, A. Jaramillo, D. F. Lane, J. F. Pacheco, M. B. Robbins, F. G. Stiles, and K. J. Zimmer. Version 30 January 2023. A classification of the bird species of South America. American Ornithological Society. https://www.museum.lsu.edu/~Remsen/SACCBaseline.htm retrieved January 30, 2023</ref> The Tucuman amazon is [[monotypic]].<ref name=IOC13.1/>

==Description==

The Tucumán amazon is about {{convert|31|cm|in|abbr=on}} long and weighs {{convert|250|to|280|g|oz|abbr=on}}. It is mostly green, with black edges on the body feathers that give a scaly appearance. Its forehead and sometimes its [[Lore (anatomy)|lores]] are red. Bare white skin surrounds its eye. Its lower thighs are orange-yellow, its primary [[Covert feather|coverts]] red, and its undertail coverts yellow-green. Its [[Primaries (birds)|primaries]] have blue tips and its tail feathers yellowish tips. Immature birds have entirely green thighs.<ref name=TUPA-BOW>Collar, N., P. F. D. Boesman, and E. de Juana (2020). Tucuman Parrot (''Amazona tucumana''), version 1.0. In Birds of the World (J. del Hoyo, A. Elliott, J. Sargatal, D. A. Christie, and E. de Juana, Editors). Cornell Lab of Ornithology, Ithaca, NY, USA. https://doi.org/10.2173/bow.tucpar1.01 retrieved February 28, 2023</ref>

==Distribution and habitat==

The Tucumán amazon is found from [[Santa Cruz Department (Bolivia)|Santa Cruz]] and [[Chuquisaca Department|Chuquisaca]] departments in Bolivia south into Argentina as far as [[Catamarca Province]]. It inhabits the [[Yungas]] bioregion in montane woodland characterized by pure stands of alder (''[[Alnus acuminata]]'') or ''[[Podocarpus parlatorei]]''. In elevation it mostly ranges between {{convert|1600|and|2600|m|ft|abbr=on}} but occurs as low as {{convert|500|m|ft|abbr=on}}.<ref name=TUPA-BOW/>

==Behavior==
===Movement===

The Tucumán amazon moves seasonally between higher elevations and, in August to October, as low as {{convert|500|m|ft|abbr=on}} at the [[Gran Chaco|Chaco]]/Yungas [[ecotone]].<ref name=TUPA-BOW/>

===Feeding===

A study in Argentina found that almost a quarter of the Tucumán amazon's diet was the fruits and seeds of ''Podocarpus parlatorei''; most of the rest was taken from six other plants. Winter food was dominated by ''[[Acacia]]'' seeds. Flowers and fruits of both native and introduced plants were eaten.<ref name=TUPA-BOW/><ref name=Rivera2012>Rivera, L., Politi, N. and Bucher, E.H. (2012). Ecología y conservación del Loro Alisero (''Amazona tucumana''). El Hornero. 27(1): 51–61.</ref>

===Breeding===

The Tucumán amazon nests between November and January. It uses cavities in large trees, often those excavated by woodpeckers. In the Argentina study, the average clutch was 3.6 eggs and ranged from one to five. The incubation period was 26 to 30 days and fledging occurred 50 to 58 days after hatch. In another study, nest success was related to the availability of ''Podocarpus parlatorei'' fruits.<ref name=TUPA-BOW/><ref name=Rivera2012/><ref name=Rivera2006>{{cite web|title=Ecology, Reproductive Biology and Conservation of Alder Amazon (''Amazona tucumana'') in the Montane Forests of Argentina|url=http://www.ruffordsmallgrants.org/rsg/Projects/LuisRivera|first=Luis|last=Rivera|year=2006|publisher=The Rufford Foundation|access-date=2008-02-24|archive-date=2011-07-27|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110727224948/http://www.ruffordsmallgrants.org/rsg/Projects/LuisRivera|url-status=dead}}</ref>

{{birdsong|url=https://xeno-canto.org/species/Amazona-tucumana |species=Tucuman amazon}}
===Vocalization===

The Tucumán amazon makes a "variety of screeches and calls, including shrill shrieks and lower-pitched barks." Its flight call is "a repeated, somewhat yelping, shrill "quiowk"."<ref name=TUPA-BOW/>

==Status==

The [[IUCN]] originally assessed the Tucumán amazon in 2004 as being of Least Concern, then in 2005 as Near Threatened, and since 2011 as Vulnerable. It has a somewhat limited range and its estimated population of fewer than 15,000 mature individuals is believed to be decreasing. "The species is susceptible to trapping for trade, which is considered the main driver of rapid population declines". "Habitat in Argentina is highly degraded and consists of small, isolated fragments."<ref name=IUCN/><ref name=Rivera2006/><ref name=Rivera2014>{{cite journal |last1=Rivera |first1=L. |last2=Bucher |first2=E.H.|last3=Pidgeon|first3=A.|title=Nesting success and productivity of Tucumán Parrots (''Amazona tucumana'') in high-altitude forests of Argentina: do they differ from lowland Amazona parrots? |journal=Emu - Austral Ornithology |date=2014 |volume=114 |issue=1 |pages=41–49 |doi=10.1071/MU12062|s2cid=10689958 |hdl=11336/1289 |hdl-access=free }}</ref> It is considered locally common but generally uncommon. It occurs in several protected areas, but less than 25% of its preferred habitat is protected.<ref name=TUPA-BOW/>

zh.extract:
榿木亞馬遜鸚鵡（Amazona tucumana），又名榿木亞馬遜鸚哥，是分佈在阿根廷及玻利維亞的鸚鵡。牠們棲息在亞熱帶或熱帶潮濕低地森林及山區。牠們失去棲息地。


參考


外部連結
Ecology, Reproductive Biology and Conservation of Alder Amazon (Amazona tucumana) in the Montane Forests of Argentina
